The user wants to create a Shopify website called "Buy Property Services" selling various property-related add-ons/services. They've provided a mockup with a green and orange color palette. Let me look at the mockup more carefully:

Colors from the mockup:
- Dark green: ~#1A5C2A or similar dark forest green
- Orange: ~#F5A623 or similar warm orange
- Lime/olive green: ~#7AB317 or similar
- Beige/cream: ~#F5F0E1 or similar
- Light gray/taupe: ~#D4CFC7 or similar


Now I'm reviewing the eight products they want to sell—everything from auction legal packs and RICS surveys to photography services and rental agreement templates. I'll need to build a custom Shopify theme using Liquid templates to match their mockup design and accommodate this product range.

I'm mapping out the core theme structure with the essential files: the main layout template, homepage and product pages, collection views for browsing, cart functionality, plus reusable sections and snippets to keep things modular. I'll also need configuration files for settings and localization. I'm thinking about a forest green color scheme to give it a professional real estate feel.

Building the Shopify theme structure... I'm also accounting for the additional configuration files, localization settings, reusable snippets for product cards and icons, and a JavaScript file for interactivity. Since I'm working from the Program Files directory, I should create these theme files in a more appropriate location that's easier to manage and package for upload to Shopify.

I'll set up the theme structure in the user's home directory at `C:\Users\colby\buy-property-theme\` to keep everything organized and accessible. Now I'm planning out the color palette for the theme—starting with a dark forest green as the primary color, warm orange for secondary accents, and a lime green for highlights, paired with a clean white background and cream alternative.

I'm also mapping out the eight core services the theme needs to showcase: auction legal documentation, professional surveys, property cost management, photography services, title deed handling, rental management, tenancy agreements, and listing promotion across major property platforms. Now I'm ready to start building out all the theme files with clean, professional Shopify code.

Building the theme structure... I'm seeing the rest of the directory structure with snippets and templates, then moving into actually writing out the file contents starting with the CSS and layout files. Let me build out the theme styles and HTML structure comprehensively. Mapping out the service icons for each offering, then I need to write all the theme files. I realize I should save these to the user's home directory rather than Program Files since that's a protected system folder, so I'll create them in a dedicated theme folder on the desktop instead.

Writing theme files... Now I'm laying out the complete theme structure and pulling together the color palette from the mockup to ensure consistency across all the files I'm about to create. I'm mapping out the page structures now—the homepage needs an announcement bar, header, hero section, services grid, testimonials, and footer, while product and collection pages follow similar patterns with their own specific content blocks. Now I'll start writing the actual code, beginning with the core layout file, stylesheets, and JavaScript, then moving through the sections and templates systematically. I've mapped out the 21 files needed for the theme structure, and now I'm ready to start writing them out. I need to follow Shopify's exact directory conventions for the ZIP upload to work properly. I'll tackle the foundational files first—the CSS and layout—then move through the sections, snippets, and configuration files in parallel batches.

Writing the stylesheet with theme variables and component styles... Setting up the spacing scale, shadow depths, and transition timing along with some layout constraints like max width and header height, then resetting default browser styles across all elements.

Now I'm establishing the core typography system with responsive heading sizes that scale fluidly based on viewport width, while also configuring base styles for the body, images, links, and lists.

Still writing styles... Still writing button styles... Still writing styles... Still writing styles... Still writing styles... Writing the hero section styles... Still writing styles... Finishing up the utility classes for accessibility and spacing, then moving on to write out all the Shopify theme files in batches—CSS and JavaScript first, then the layout and section templates in parallel groups for efficiency.

I'm planning the file structure carefully, making sure the Liquid syntax is correct for Shopify-specific tags. For the service cards and product pages, I'll embed SVG icons directly in the sections rather than trying to manage a complex icon mapping system, which will keep things simpler and more maintainable.

Now I'm ready to start writing out all the theme files. I'll create the directory structure first, then write each file systematically starting from the base path.

Search the current working directory (C:\Program Files) and its immediate subdirectories for any project files that indicate dev servers. Look for:

1. package.json files (check "scripts" for dev/start/serve commands)
2. pyproject.toml, setup.py, manage.py (Django/Flask/FastAPI)
3. Pipfile, requirements.txt (look for flask, uvicorn, gunicorn, django)
4. Makefile (look for dev/serve/start targets)
5. docker-compose.yml / docker-compose.yaml (look for services with ports)
6. vite.config.js/ts, next.config.js, nuxt.config.js, angular.json, vue.config.js
7. Cargo.toml (Rust), go.mod (Go), mix.exs (Elixir/Phoenix)
8. .env or .env.example files (look for PORT variables)
9. Procfile (Heroku-style)
10. Any config files that mention ports or dev server commands

For each file found, read its contents and extract:
- The command to run the dev server
- The port it runs on (if specified)
- The name/type of the server

Return ALL findings with file paths and exact content of relevant sections. Be thorough - check subdirectories too.

cd "C:/Users/colby/buy-property-theme" && powershell -Command "
\$source = Get-Location
\$dest = 'C:/Users/colby/buy-property-theme.zip'
if (Test-Path \$dest) { Remove-Item \$dest }
\$files = Get-ChildItem -Recurse -File
\$zip = [System.IO.Compression.ZipFile]::Open(\$dest, 'Create')
Add-Type -Assembly 'System.IO.Compression.FileSystem'
foreach (\$file in \$files) {
  \$entryName = \$file.FullName.Substring(\$source.Path.Length + 1).Replace('\', '/')
  [System.IO.Compression.ZipFileExtensions]::CreateEntryFromFile(\$zip, \$file.FullName, \$entryName) | Out-Null
}
\$zip.Dispose()
Write-Host 'Done'
"
